title: Capitol Remembrance Act
plain_summary: This bill requires the person in charge of maintaining the Capitol building to create a permanent display about the January 6, 2021 attack on the Capitol. The display must be put in an easy-to-see location and include damaged pieces from the building, photos from that day, and a special sign honoring the police officers and other people who protected the Capitol. The display must also honor those who died or got hurt defending the building and the workers who helped fix it afterward. The whole display must be finished and put up within two years after this bill becomes law.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Capitol Remembrance Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Exhibit Depicting the Attack on the Capitol
sections.text: The person in charge of maintaining the Capitol building (called the Architect of the Capitol) must create and put up a permanent display within two years of this law being passed. This display will show what happened during the attack on the Capitol building on January sixth, two thousand twenty one. The Architect must work with a group called the Joint Committee on the Library to plan this display. The display must be placed in an important location inside the Capitol building where people can easily see it. The Architect should try to save and include pieces of the Capitol building or grounds that were damaged during the attack if this is possible. The display must include photographs that already exist showing the attack. There must be a special sign or plaque that honors three groups of people. First, it honors the Capitol Police and other police officers who protected the Capitol building that day. Second, it honors the heroes who died or were hurt while protecting the Capitol, including Capitol Police Officers Brian Sicknick and Howard Liebengood, Metropolitan Police Officers Jeffrey Smith, Gunther Hashida, and Kyle DeFreytag, and others who were injured. Third, it honors the Capitol staff workers who helped fix and clean up the Capitol building after the attack. The Architect can also include artwork that shows the attack if they want to. Congress can provide whatever money is needed to pay for this project. Any money given for this project can be used until it is all spent, even if that takes several years.
